content.text: Model Routing and Fallback ADR Patterns

Enterprise Claude integrations rarely route every request to a single model. Different requests have different complexity, latency, and cost profiles — and the optimal model for each varies. Model routing is the architectural pattern that dispatches requests to the most appropriate model tier based on request characteristics. Fallback is the complementary pattern that redirects a request to an alternative when the primary model fails, is unavailable, or exceeds a latency threshold.

Both routing and fallback require an Architecture Decision Record (ADR) because they embed lasting policy into the system: which model handles which request type, what triggers a fallback, and what the fallback sequence is. These decisions determine cost, quality, and reliability at scale and must be documented so they can be reviewed, challenged, and updated as the model landscape evolves.

attrs.alt: Model routing and fallback decision flow: classify request complexity, route to Haiku/Sonnet/Opus primary, fall back to alternative on failure

content.text: Why Routing and Fallback Deserve Separate ADRs
Routing and fallback are distinct concerns that produce distinct architectural consequences:
Routing
is a proactive, quality-driven decision made before the API call. It answers: given what we know about this request, which model best balances quality and cost?
Fallback
is a reactive, reliability-driven decision made after a failure. It answers: given that the primary path failed, what is the safest alternative that preserves the user experience?

Conflating them in a single ADR produces a document that is difficult to reason about and difficult to update when either the routing logic or the failure handling changes independently. The patterns below present each as a standalone ADR template, to be used together or separately depending on the integration.
ADR: Request Routing Policy
When to Write This ADR
Write a Request Routing Policy ADR when:
The integration will serve requests with meaningfully different complexity (simple lookups alongside multi-step reasoning tasks)
Cost-per-request is a budget constraint and a cheaper model is acceptable for a subset of requests
Latency requirements differ by request type (interactive queries vs. batch document processing)
The integration will be used by user populations with different needs (a helpdesk tier using Haiku, an analysis tier using Opus)

ADR-XXX: Request Routing Policy for [Integration Name]
Status: Proposed | Accepted | Superseded
Date: YYYY-MM-DD
Deciders: [Architecture lead, Product owner, Operations lead]
Supersedes: [prior ADR number if applicable]

CONTEXT
[Integration name] will receive requests that vary significantly in:
  - Complexity: simple factual lookups, standard analytical queries, complex
    multi-step reasoning or creative generation
  - Latency requirements: interactive (P95 < 5s) vs. batch (minutes acceptable)
  - Token footprint: short context (< 2k tokens) vs. long context (> 50k tokens)

A single-model policy routes all requests to [model], producing unnecessary cost
for simple requests and potentially insufficient quality for complex ones.

DECISION
Requests are classified at ingress using a lightweight classifier before the
primary API call. The classifier assigns each request one of three tiers:

  TIER 1 — simple
    Criteria: no multi-step reasoning required; factual retrieval or
    short-form generation; context < 4k tokens; historical P95 latency < 2s
    Examples: "summarise this paragraph", "what is the status of order X"
    Model: claude-haiku-4-5-20251001
    Max tokens: 512
    Cost target: < $0.002 per request

  TIER 2 — standard  (default for unclassified requests)
    Criteria: analytical reasoning; moderate context (4k–30k tokens);
    structured output generation; multi-turn conversation continuation
    Examples: contract clause analysis, code review, document Q&A
    Model: claude-sonnet-4-6
    Max tokens: 2048
    Cost target: < $0.03 per request

  TIER 3 — complex
    Criteria: multi-step strategic reasoning; long context > 30k tokens;
    output quality is the primary constraint; batch processing acceptable
    Examples: full-document legal review, architecture design, root-cause analysis
    Model: claude-opus-4-8
    Max tokens: 8192
    Cost target: < $0.50 per request

CLASSIFIER IMPLEMENTATION
The classifier is a rule-based function applied before the API call:

  1. Token count: count input tokens using the tokenisation library.
     If > 30k tokens → TIER 3 regardless of other signals.
     If < 4k tokens → candidate for TIER 1; apply further checks.
  2. Intent signals: inspect system prompt and user message for tier markers
     defined in the intent-signal registry (see docs/routing/intent-signals.md).
     Tier markers are explicit classification signals added by the calling
     application when the nature of the request is known at call time.
  3. Default: any request not classified by the above rules → TIER 2.

The classifier does NOT call the Claude API. It uses the tiktoken library
for token counting and a keyword/regex match for intent signals.
Classifier latency budget: < 10ms P99.

CONSEQUENCES
  Positive:
    - Projected 40% reduction in average cost per request vs. single-model (Sonnet) policy
    - Tier-1 requests meet a lower latency target (Haiku is ~2× faster than Sonnet)
    - Routing logic is auditable and can be adjusted without application code changes
      (intent-signal registry is configuration, not code)
  Negative:
    - Classifier introduces a dependency: misclassification routes a complex request
      to Haiku, producing degraded quality. The first 30 days of production will
      include a quality audit of tier-1 responses to detect systematic misclassification.
    - Three model configurations to maintain (system prompts, max_tokens, cost
      tracking) rather than one.
    - Tier boundary decisions are subjective. This ADR records the initial thresholds;
      they will be revisited at the 30-day operational review.

ALTERNATIVES CONSIDERED
  A. Static routing by user role (admin → Opus, agent → Sonnet, end-user → Haiku):
     Rejected. Role does not correlate reliably with request complexity in this integration.
  B. LLM-based classifier (use Claude to classify each request before routing):
     Rejected. Adds ~$0.002–0.005 per request to classify; eliminates most of the
     cost saving from routing. May be revisited if rule-based classifier accuracy
     proves insufficient.
  C. Single model (Sonnet) for all requests:
     Rejected. Cost modelling shows 40% overspend on tier-1 requests at production volume.

IMPLEMENTATION NOTES
  - Classifier: src/routing/classifier.py
  - Intent-signal registry: docs/routing/intent-signals.md
  - Per-tier cost tracking: use the metadata field in the API response to tag
    requests with their tier for cost dashboard breakdown.
  - Monitoring: alert if tier-1 share drops below 15% or above 50% of total
    requests (signals classifier drift or usage pattern shift).

REVIEW DATE: [date + 90 days from acceptance]
ADR: Fallback and Retry Policy
When to Write This ADR
Write a Fallback and Retry Policy ADR when:
The integration requires higher than baseline availability (standard Claude API availability is 99.9%)
The integration is customer-facing with a latency SLA that the primary model may occasionally miss
Multiple model tiers create multiple potential failure points with different fallback sequences
Cost and quality tradeoffs in fallback situations need to be explicitly decided (not left to the on-call engineer)

ADR-XXX: Fallback and Retry Policy for [Integration Name]
Status: Proposed | Accepted | Superseded
Date: YYYY-MM-DD
Deciders: [Architecture lead, Operations lead, Product owner]
Related: ADR-XXX (Request Routing Policy)

CONTEXT
[Integration name] routes requests to three model tiers (see routing ADR).
Each tier can fail in distinct ways:
  - API errors: 5xx responses from the Anthropic API (rate limits, service errors)
  - Latency violations: P95 response time exceeds the tier's latency budget
  - Content filtering: the API returns a stop_reason of "content_filter"

Without an explicit fallback policy, failures are either silently returned to the
user as errors, or handled inconsistently by different parts of the application code.

DECISION
The fallback policy defines, for each failure mode and each tier, the sequence of
actions to take before returning an error to the user.

FAILURE MODE 1: API Error (4xx/5xx)

  TIER 1 (Haiku) API error:
    1. Retry the same request on Haiku with exponential backoff: 1s, 2s, 4s
       (max 3 retries, total retry window: 7s)
    2. If all retries fail: escalate to Tier 2 (Sonnet), same request, one attempt
    3. If Sonnet also fails: return a structured error to the caller with
       error_code: "MODEL_UNAVAILABLE" and retry_after: 30

  TIER 2 (Sonnet) API error:
    1. Retry Sonnet with exponential backoff: 2s, 4s, 8s (max 3 retries)
    2. If all retries fail: return error_code: "MODEL_UNAVAILABLE" and retry_after: 60
    Note: do NOT fall back to Haiku for a Tier-2 request — quality degradation
    from Sonnet to Haiku is unacceptable for standard-complexity requests in this integration.

  TIER 3 (Opus) API error:
    1. Retry Opus with exponential backoff: 5s, 10s, 20s (max 3 retries)
    2. If all retries fail: escalate to Tier 2 (Sonnet), one attempt
    3. If Sonnet also fails: queue the request for deferred processing (Tier-3
       requests are batch-acceptable per the routing ADR); notify the user
       that the result will be delivered asynchronously.

FAILURE MODE 2: Latency Violation

  A latency violation occurs when the API call does not return within the
  tier latency budget. The integration uses a per-tier timeout:
    Tier 1: 8s timeout (2× the P95 target, allowing headroom)
    Tier 2: 30s timeout
    Tier 3: 120s timeout (batch acceptable)

  On timeout:
    Tier 1: cancel the in-flight request; escalate to Sonnet (one attempt, 30s timeout);
            if Sonnet also times out → return error_code: "LATENCY_EXCEEDED"
    Tier 2: cancel; return error_code: "LATENCY_EXCEEDED" (do not fall back to Haiku)
    Tier 3: do not timeout; allow up to 300s before returning an async queue response

FAILURE MODE 3: Content Filtering

  If stop_reason == "content_filter":
    1. Log the full request (system prompt + user message) to the content-filter
       audit log (encrypted, access-controlled, retained 90 days)
    2. Return a user-facing message: "This request could not be completed.
       Please rephrase your question or contact support."
    3. Do NOT retry or fall back to another model — content filtering is
       a policy signal, not a transient error.
    4. Trigger an alert to the operations team for review within 24 hours.

RATE LIMIT HANDLING

  HTTP 429 (rate limit) is treated differently from other API errors:
    - Do not retry immediately (that will produce another 429)
    - Read the retry-after header and wait exactly that duration before one retry
    - If the retry also returns 429: enqueue the request (Tier 1 and Tier 3)
      or return error_code: "RATE_LIMITED" with the retry_after value (Tier 2)
    - Alert operations if 429 rate exceeds 5% of requests in any 5-minute window

CONSEQUENCES
  Positive:
    - Explicit policy eliminates ad-hoc error handling across application code
    - Tier-1 and Tier-3 fallbacks preserve user experience for most failures
    - Content-filter audit log supports compliance requirements
    - Rate-limit handling prevents thundering-herd retry storms
  Negative:
    - Fallback from Tier 1 to Tier 2 increases cost for those requests (Tier-2 cost
      applies when fallback is invoked); this is acceptable given the low frequency
      of API errors
    - Tier-2 requests have no quality-preserving fallback (deliberate decision)
    - Deferred-processing path for Tier-3 failures requires queue infrastructure
      (see infrastructure ADR ADR-XXX)

ALTERNATIVES CONSIDERED
  A. Retry-only policy (no escalation): rejected. Tier-1 errors during an API
     degradation incident could last minutes; retrying Haiku 3× produces
     user-facing latency of 7+ seconds with no resolution.
  B. Always fall back to Sonnet: rejected. Unnecessary for Tier-3 requests where
     async delivery is acceptable; and Sonnet is not acceptable quality for
     complex requests that specifically required Opus routing.
  C. Circuit breaker pattern (stop sending to a model after N failures): deferred.
     Will be added in a follow-up ADR once failure rate baselines are established
     from 30 days of production data.

IMPLEMENTATION NOTES
  - Retry logic: src/api/retry.py (uses tenacity library)
  - Timeout enforcement: asyncio timeout context around each API call
  - Content-filter audit log: CloudWatch Logs group /learnwithdeiva/content-filter
  - Queue for deferred Tier-3 processing: SQS queue claude-tier3-deferred
  - Dashboard: per-tier error rate, fallback invocation rate, 429 rate

REVIEW DATE: [date + 90 days from acceptance]
Implementing the Classifier

The routing classifier is the most operationally significant component of this architecture — a systematic misclassification affects cost and quality at every request. The classifier must be testable and its accuracy must be measured in production.

# src/routing/classifier.py
from dataclasses import dataclass
from enum import IntEnum
import re

class Tier(IntEnum):
    SIMPLE = 1
    STANDARD = 2
    COMPLEX = 3

TIER_CONFIG = {
    Tier.SIMPLE:   {"model": "claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",  "max_tokens": 512,  "timeout_s": 8},
    Tier.STANDARD: {"model": "claude-sonnet-4-6",           "max_tokens": 2048, "timeout_s": 30},
    Tier.COMPLEX:  {"model": "claude-opus-4-8",              "max_tokens": 8192, "timeout_s": 120},
}

# Intent signals registered by calling applications to force a tier
COMPLEX_INTENT_SIGNALS = frozenset([
    "full_document_review", "strategic_analysis", "architecture_design",
    "root_cause_analysis", "legal_review",
])
SIMPLE_INTENT_SIGNALS = frozenset([
    "status_lookup", "short_summary", "field_extraction", "faq_response",
])

@dataclass
class RoutingDecision:
    tier: Tier
    reason: str
    model: str
    max_tokens: int
    timeout_s: int

def classify(
    system_prompt: str,
    user_message: str,
    intent_signal: str | None = None,
    token_count: int | None = None,
) -> RoutingDecision:
    # 1. Explicit intent signal (highest priority)
    if intent_signal in COMPLEX_INTENT_SIGNALS:
        t = Tier.COMPLEX
        reason = f"intent_signal:{intent_signal}"
    elif intent_signal in SIMPLE_INTENT_SIGNALS:
        t = Tier.SIMPLE
        reason = f"intent_signal:{intent_signal}"
    # 2. Token count
    elif token_count is not None and token_count > 30_000:
        t = Tier.COMPLEX
        reason = f"token_count:{token_count}"
    elif token_count is not None and token_count < 4_000:
        t = Tier.SIMPLE
        reason = f"token_count:{token_count}"
    # 3. Default
    else:
        t = Tier.STANDARD
        reason = "default"

    cfg = TIER_CONFIG[t]
    return RoutingDecision(
        tier=t, reason=reason,
        model=cfg["model"], max_tokens=cfg["max_tokens"], timeout_s=cfg["timeout_s"],
    )
Implementing Retry and Fallback

The fallback policy translates directly into an async retry wrapper. The wrapper is responsible for implementing the exact sequences defined in the ADR — it must not make ad-hoc decisions about when to escalate or return errors.
Monitoring Routing and Fallback Health

The routing and fallback policies produce observable signals that must be monitored. A routing policy that is working as designed produces a stable tier distribution over time. Drift in that distribution is a signal worth investigating.
Metric
Signal
Alert Threshold
Investigative Action
Tier-1 request share
% of total requests routed to Haiku
Review classifier logs for systematic misclassification; check for usage pattern shift
Fallback invocation rate
% of Tier-1 requests that fell back to Sonnet
> 5% in a 5-min window
Check Anthropic status page; inspect error type (5xx vs. timeout)
429 rate per tier
% of requests receiving a rate limit response
> 2% sustained
Review API tier capacity; implement request queuing if not already in place
Content filter rate
% of requests returning content_filter stop reason
> 0.1% or any cluster
Review audit log; check for prompt injection attempts; assess system prompt scope
Fallback cost delta
Cost of fallback-invoked requests vs. primary
Monthly cost > 5% of budget
Review whether Tier-1 thresholds are too aggressive; reduce or restructure classifier
ADR Maintenance for Routing Decisions

Model routing ADRs require more frequent review than most architectural decisions because the model landscape changes. New model releases alter the cost/quality frontier; price changes shift the tier economics; usage pattern evolution may invalidate the classifier's thresholds.
30-day review:
Compare actual tier distribution against the projected distribution from the ADR. If actual Tier-1 share is outside the projected range, investigate classifier accuracy before the 90-day review.
90-day review:
Full review of tier thresholds, cost targets, and fallback sequences against 90 days of production data. Update the ADR if any threshold changes are accepted.
Model release review:

When Anthropic releases a new model in any tier, trigger an out-of-cycle ADR review. The new model may offer better cost/quality at an existing tier, or may introduce a new tier that the current routing policy does not account for.
Price change review:
Token price changes shift the cost targets in the ADR. A price reduction may make a higher tier economically viable as the default; a price increase may require tighter tier boundaries.

The worst routing policy failure mode is not misclassification — it is invisible misclassification. A classifier that silently routes complex requests to Haiku will degrade user experience while cost metrics show apparent improvement. The quality audit during the first 30 days of production, sampling Tier-1 responses for quality review, is the primary defence against this failure.
Summary

Model routing and fallback are complementary architectural patterns that together control the cost, quality, and reliability of a Claude integration at scale. Routing is a proactive quality decision: classify the request before the API call and send it to the model tier that best matches its complexity. Fallback is a reactive reliability decision: when the primary path fails, follow a predefined escalation sequence rather than returning an error.

Both patterns require explicit ADRs because they embed policy into the system architecture. The ADRs define the tier boundaries, the fallback sequences, the timeout values, and the monitoring thresholds — decisions that would otherwise be made ad hoc by application code or on-call engineers. Documenting them as ADRs creates a reviewable record that can be updated as the model landscape changes and as production data informs better threshold choices.
